lines: **Why registry-level operations get parallel cult verbs per side, not shared verbs.** Made-side `abjure` and fetched-side `expel` share one registry primitive (`packages delete`), but the noun-split keeps them as parallel verbs — distinct name, colophon, spec, and surface vocabulary, with the shared mechanism living in registry-utility code. The analogy carries through verbs; it does not collapse at the substrate. A future session tempted to unify because "the code is identical" should resist — the parallelism is load-bearing for the model even where the implementation is not.

-**Why capture and election are separate layers — no mixing of the GAR copy with the install into a final resting place.** Capture (a `bank`/`sheathe`/… verb) is **capture-pure**: it mints the Lode, emits the signed provenance envelope, resolves and *reports* the touchmark — and writes **no consumer config**. Election (where a consumer pins a touchmark) is a separate consumption-side layer that *reads* what capture emitted. The interface between the layers is the **capture-file**: one provenance fact-file per capture, in the existing `rbf_fact_*` presence-as-fact idiom (NOT one mega-JSON, NOT per-aspect explosion — one file per captured touchmark, the roster precedent). This is the prototype's brand-file promoted from "unsigned text written at consumption" to "signed artifact emitted at acquisition." The payoff is concrete: election *chains* off the capture-file, retries independently, and **survives an election failure** (the Lode + file persist); and a fan-out election (one touchmark → N vessels) iterates by *reading the file*, not by a verb taking N vessel arguments. The made side already half-violates this for ergonomics (the kludge cycle drives a hallmark into a nameplate in one gesture) — so the rule is at the **verb/spec** level (`bank` etc. stay pure); a *cycle tabtarget* may still bundle capture+elect for dev ergonomics, exactly as kludge does. Bundling lives in `tt/`, never in the verb's meaning.

+**Why capture and election are separate layers — no mixing of the GAR copy with the install into a final resting place.** Capture (a `bank`/`sheathe`/… verb) is **capture-pure**: it mints the Lode, emits the signed provenance envelope, resolves and *reports* the touchmark — and writes **no consumer config**. Election (where a consumer pins a touchmark) is a separate consumption-side layer that *reads* what capture emitted. The interface between the layers is the **capture-file**: one provenance fact-file per capture, in the existing `rbf_fact_*` presence-as-fact idiom (NOT one mega-JSON, NOT per-aspect explosion — one file per captured touchmark, the roster precedent). It is the brand-file-to-signed-envelope promotion named in the provenance-invariant premise, here given its concrete handoff role. The payoff is concrete: election *chains* off the capture-file, retries independently, and **survives an election failure** (the Lode + file persist); and a fan-out election (one touchmark → N vessels) iterates by *reading the file*, not by a verb taking N vessel arguments. The made side already half-violates this for ergonomics (the kludge cycle drives a hallmark into a nameplate in one gesture) — so the rule is at the **verb/spec** level (`bank` etc. stay pure); a *cycle tabtarget* may still bundle capture+elect for dev ergonomics, exactly as kludge does. Bundling lives in `tt/`, never in the verb's meaning.

**Why the per-kind intent carries no FQIN — most regression-prone principle.** When quay, docker-hub, or Canonical reorganizes upstream paths, kind-pipeline code changes; the per-kind intent declaration does not. If a future pace proposes adding resolved-coordinate detail to a kind's intent file, that is a smell — the principle is being violated. RBSRV's ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern is the precedent: ORIGIN as author intent, ANCHOR as resolved-by-pipeline (and, on the fetched side, the resolved ANCHOR *is* the elected touchmark). The Lode model generalizes that pattern, not weakens it. Each kind's upstream-source convention lives in pipeline code where it can be fixed in one place.

- **Capture is per-kind, not one verb — a deliberate reversal this session of the earlier "one verb to lode them all."** `enshrine` **retires entirely** (it survives nowhere — the made side is `conjure`/`ordain`). Each kind gets its own custodial verb so an operator speaks one word and means exactly one thing, with no kind-argument indirection. The reversal is *safe* because the unification was relocated off the verb name onto the **capture-file + the `Lode` noun** (the shared *output*): every per-kind verb emits the same-shaped provenance fact-file into the same package noun — that sameness *is* "one shape," and it is the guard that keeps per-kind *names* from becoming per-kind *code paths* (the parallel-infrastructure the model rejects). Verbs and their kinds — register is **custodial/stronghold** (each names a kind of secure store), deliberately distinct from the made side's **alchemical** `conjure`/`ordain` (a down-payment on the made/fetched side-labels, still parked):

lines: Lifecycle fixtures against the new Lode surface must be slated and landed before deploy: capture / list / inspect / wrest / delete end-to-end against live GAR per kind, bookended by prereq-sweep at head and muster-absent at tail. The bar forks by kind: `base`/`tool`/`reliquary` carry their consumption-adjacent paths; `wsl`/`podvm` stop at the registry — **capture → list → inspect → per-member + whole-Lode delete against live GAR, no host in the loop** (consumption deferred). Whole-Lode delete is a single `packages delete` — the regression risk shifts to the **per-member delete path** (the cleanup-after-bugs case): multi-member kinds must carry concrete member-absence assertions after a partial delete, since that is where a package can be left in a partial state. The fixture surface scales with the operation surface; test paces are not optional polish. Manual operator testing during planning is the bridge, not a substitute.

message.content: do you have an inclination on yoke?

content.text:
Yes — and it's clearer than I expected once I push on what yoke actually *touches*.

**My inclination: election does NOT belong in `l`. It lives with the consumer it writes to, and it's lowercase.**

The reasoning, from the principles we already locked:

**1. Election mutates the *consumer*, not the Lode.** When you yoke, you write a vessel's RELIQUARY slot. The touchmark is the *argument*; the **vessel** is the *target*. The salient-noun rule (the thing colophon families key on) points at the vessel, not the Lode — the same way `expel` *takes* a touchmark but *targets* the Lode, so it's an `l` op. Object-of-mutation decides the family, not argument type. So `yoke` is a vessel op, and the deferred substrate-election verb is a nameplate op.

**2. This is just the "election co-locates with consumption" premise, expressed at the colophon layer.** The Touchmark-election table already says base/tool/reliquary elect *in the vessel* and wsl/podvm elect *in the nameplate*. If election co-locates with the consumer conceptually, its commands should co-locate with the consumer's family too — `yoke` near vessel-regime, substrate-election near nameplate. Election *scattering* across consumer families isn't a smell; it's the honest tabtarget-level shape of "election forks per kind."

**3. It parallels the made side exactly.** A hallmark is elected into a nameplate via the nameplate/kludge machinery — *not* via an ark/hallmark family. Made-side election lives with the consumer. Fetched-side election should too. Keeping it out of `l` makes `l` mean one crisp thing: **operations that create or destroy a Lode in GAR** — capture and expel, nothing else. Maintenance is the `image` backdoor; election is consumer-side; consumption is consumption. Clean separation, and it *reinforces* the no-mixing premise at the family level (capture in `l`, election elsewhere — they don't even share a family).

**On the capital convention:** election writes cheap, reversible *config*, not persistent GAR/cost state — so by the convention's actual criterion ("persistent and cost-incurring"), it's **lowercase**. That's a second, independent reason it doesn't fit `l`: `l` is uniformly the capital GAR-touching ops, and election would be the lone lowercase misfit there. Outside `l`, in its consumer family, lowercase election sits naturally.

**The one soft spot (genuinely pace-time):** today's `yoke` is `rbw-dY` — Depot, capital Y. Both are arguably wrong under this reasoning (it's a vessel op, and it's config not GAR). So adopting this means yoke *moves out of Depot* and *de-capitalizes* on rename. The exact destination letter (vessel-regime `rbw-rv?` vs a fresh vessel family) is a layout detail I'd leave to the pace — but the **principle** (election with the consumer, lowercase, not in `l`) is what I'd lock.

Net: `l` stays small and pure — `bank`/`sheathe`/`garner`/`harbor`/`vault` + `expel`, all GAR, all capital. Want me to record this as a decided premise (with the yoke-moves-out-of-Depot consequence flagged pace-time), or sit with it first?

lastPrompt: I'm pretty inclined to follow your recommendation.  But first- any thoughts on whether this command ought to actually git commit?  I haven't done an inventory in a while, but I think we have refrained…

content.text:
Researched, and the answer is clean — **no, it should not commit, and it already doesn't.** The existing `yoke` is the direct precedent, and it's explicit in both spec and code:

- **RBSDY spec, twice:** *"This primitive does not commit; the caller (orchestrator or operator) commits the {rbrv_regime} changes."*
- **`rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh:329`:** after writing rbrv.env, it just emits *"Commit the rbrv.env changes with your usual git workflow."*

So your memory is exactly right, and it's the near-universal RBK convention, not a one-off:

**Tools write config and leave the commit to the operator.** kludge writes rbrn.env and leaves it dirty; foundry-director-build, charge, yoke — none self-commit. The *only* RBK bash command that runs `git commit` is **Marshal-Zero** (`rblm_cli.sh:240`), and that's a deliberate wholesale release-qualification *reset* ceremony — which still gates a clean tree first. Routine state-mutating tools never commit. (This serves audit integrity and the multi-officium additive-commit discipline — a tool auto-committing would entangle concurrent officia's work.)

**The dirty-tree fail-fast is a *separate*, purpose-specific guard — applied by need, not bundled with committing:**
- kludge / mirror gate clean *before building* so the artifact's `git describe` ties to a commit;
- charge gates the *nameplate* clean because it consumes committed state (`rbob_bottle.sh:423`);
- graft explicitly has **no** guard — *"git state is irrelevant to an already-built container"* (RBSAG).

So the gate lives at the operation that needs committed-state correspondence, not at the writer.

**How this maps onto our new model — and it sharpens the capture/election split a third way:**

- **Capture verbs (`bank`/`sheathe`/…) have no git dimension at all.** They write the Lode + capture-file to **GAR**, not git — and, by no-mixing, they write zero vessel config. Nothing git to commit. (Capital colophon, GAR/cost-bearing.)
- **Election (`yoke` + the deferred substrate verb) is the only layer that touches git-tracked config** (vessel rbrv.env / nameplate). And it follows the convention exactly: **write the config, emit a "commit with your usual workflow" hint, do not commit, do not self-gate.** The downstream consumer (the build that reads the vessel; charge that reads the nameplate) is where the clean-tree gate already lives.

This actually *reinforces* the placement inclination from before. Election is now distinguished from `l`-family capture on three consistent axes: it targets the **consumer** (not the Lode), it's **lowercase** (cheap reversible config, not GAR/cost), and it's **operator-committed** (config write, the standard RBK refrain) — whereas capture targets GAR, is capital, and has no git question. Three axes all pointing the same way: election is its own consumer-side category, not an `l` op.

So, to fold it all together as the election decision:
- `yoke` (vessel) + substrate-election (nameplate) — **out of `l`, with their consumers; lowercase; do not commit** (write config, hint the operator), **do not self-gate** (downstream build/charge gates clean).
- The yoke-moves-out-of-Depot-`d` and de-capitalize-on-rename consequences stay pace-time.

Want me to record this as a short *Carried forward* note (election placement + lowercase + no-commit + downstream-gates), so the loose thread closes and a pace inherits it?

message.content: yes, but don't slate paces yet...

content.text: On it — recording the election decision, no paces.

118	## Carried forward / consequences
119	
120	- **caparison-w / garrison-w for the `wsl` kind.** The workload's `rbtww-main` is registered via `wsl --import` from a seed tarball; today that seed is created by `wsl --export` of admin's installed distro (GarrisonWsl). Under the `wsl` kind, that export path retires — the seed becomes a verified artifact pulled from GAR. The pre-existing revert pace in ₣A- targeting the WSL-stage DEV CACHE shortcut becomes obsolete and wants dropping or transferring when this heat commits to development.
121	- **Windows onboarding is stale and deferred.** The `rbhw*` Windows handbook tracks (Docker Desktop, context discipline) are stale and incomplete, and `wsl`/`podvm` consumption is deferred — so their onboarding has nothing live to teach. Lean: mark those tracks loudly NOT-available / deferred rather than delete, so the eventual `wsl`-kind consumption work rereads the host-side prose rather than reconstructing it. Onboarding follows *consumption*, not *acquisition*: the Lode acquisition unification does NOT unify the onboarding surface; substrate-kind onboarding lands in the host/Windows tracks, never in the Director build-lifecycle tracks.
122	- **`inscribe` is verb-overloaded — disambiguate, don't conflate.** `rbfl_inscribe` (reliquary tool-mirror, `rbw-dI`) is **in scope** — it is the operation absorbed into the per-kind capture verbs on the `tool`/`reliquary` kinds (`sheathe`/`garner`). `rbrd_inscribe` (depot tripwire, `rbw-rdi`) is a **different operation, out of scope, stays put**. (Strike the earlier "the `mirror` verb retires" wording: at quoin level there is no mirror verb — `rbfd_mirror` is the bind vessel-mode copy, made-side, also out of scope. What is absorbed is `inscribe`/reliquary, not "mirror.")
123	- **Onboarding rename surface (tri-surfaced vs greenfield).** `base`/`tool`/`reliquary` are fully present across bash, adoc, and the Director onboarding tracks (`rbw-Odf`/`Oda`/`Odb`) — for these the cost is *rename* (only two typed linked terms, `RBYC_ENSHRINE` + `RBYC_RELIQUARY`, drive the typed references; the rest is hand-edited prose). `wsl`/`podvm-*` are surfaced *nowhere* today — for these the cost is *greenfield*, not rename. Do not estimate the onboarding work as uniform.
124	- **Refresh cadence** is per-kind and may diverge (podvm-wsl vs podvm-native).
125	- **Maintenance verbs stay in the `image` family (`rbw-i*`), deliberately.** `rekon` / `audit` / `wrest` / `jettison` survive intact and do **not** move into `rbw-l*`. They are a low-level backdoor — primitives for cleaning up bad things at the member/tag grain — and that's where they belong; the `l` family is for the first-class Lode lifecycle. Their fetched-side domain variants (Reliquary + Enshrinement) consolidate under Lode/touchmark addressing; the made-side Hallmark variants are out of scope, untouched.
126	- **Tabtarget shape is decided, not open:** per-kind capture verbs under the new `l` (Lode) command family — `rbw-lB/lS/lG/lH/lV` capture, `rbw-lE` expel — superseding the earlier "unified `rbw-dE` with a kind argument" framing. (These move out of the Depot `d` family where `enshrine`/`inscribe`/`yoke` live today.)
127	
128	## Discovery recipes — work to enumerate when paces approach
