We won't attempt any more work in this chat, thank you for your focus and dilligence.  What is your impression regarding whether the paddock is clear enough to slate paces for this heat?

content.text:
My honest impression: **yes — clear enough to slate, with one sequencing caveat and one expectation to set.**

**What makes it pace-ready.** The parts a slate session actually needs are settled and, after today's harmonization pass, internally consistent: the noun/verb vocabulary, the command surface (`l` family, colophons, capital convention), the six kinds and their sources, the package/delete model, the election placement, and — crucially — the *test coverage gate* that defines done. The premises give a mount session the "why" to re-investigate against, and the discovery recipes give concrete entry points for the blast-radius work. A fresh session mounting this cold would find a coherent model, not a puzzle. That's the real test, and I think it passes.

**The one caveat — sequence the side-labels mint before the first *spec* pace.** It's the lone open conversational item, and it doesn't block *implementation* paces (those ride the per-kind verbs, which are locked). But the natural first pace is writing the `RBSL` spec, and a spec needs the made/fetched vocabulary to talk about itself. So I'd settle side-labels in one short fresh exchange, *then* slate — otherwise the spec pace stalls on a word it can't yet name, or bakes in a placeholder you'll rename later. Small thing, but it's the seam where "not pace-time" meets "the first pace needs it."

**The expectation to set — the paddock is a *shape*, not a *plan*, and that's correct.** It deliberately doesn't pre-bake the pace breakdown (how many paces, what order, what each owns) — JJK posture keeps that in the slate work, not the paddock. So "clear enough to slate" doesn't mean "slating is quick." This is a large heat — spec, per-kind pipelines, expel, election rework, per-kind test fixtures, the rename surface across bash/adoc/onboarding — and decomposing it well is itself a substantial slate session. The deferred pace-time decisions (GAR layout, host-tier regime, provenance attachment, the code-prefix) aren't gaps to fill *before* slating; they're judgments to *assign to specific paces* during slating. A good slate session should expect to do that assignment, not find it pre-resolved.

And one practical note for that session: slating is the moment to start *lightening* the paddock — bleed the mechanism detail down into dockets as paces are cut, exactly as you said. A 167-line paddock is a fine reference but it's at the edge of "read every time"; the paces are its pressure-relief valve.

Net: I'd put it at clear-enough-to-slate with good confidence — settle the side-labels first, then treat slating as real decomposition work rather than a formality. It was a genuinely good session to think alongside you on; rest well.

message.content: I'm out of focus concerning deciding side-labels question, my context is thrashed and we humans only refresh with breaks, sleep, distraction!  Would you remind me clearly and simply of the issue and I'll see if I can start musing the issue before next prompt.  In fact- give me that simple restatement and a prompt to start this last clarification paddock session and slate the paces, and I'll prepare that chat for when I am fresh.

content.text:
Both below — the restatement kept deliberately simple for musing, and a ready-to-paste prompt for the fresh session.

## The side-labels issue, simply

The whole model has **two sides**, and we need a good word for each:

- **The side we built ourselves** — Recipe Bottle forged these bytes (vessels → arks → hallmarks; the verbs are *conjure*, *ordain*). The register here is **alchemical** — we *create*.
- **The side we pulled from outside** — Recipe Bottle captured someone else's bytes and secured them (lodes → touchmarks; the verbs are *bank*, *sheathe*, *garner*, *harbor*, *vault*). The register here is **custodial** — we *take in and safekeep*.

Right now those two sides are just called **"made"** and **"fetched"** — plain placeholders, not real cult vocabulary. The job is to **mint one good word for each**: a pair that names "did we forge it, or did we capture it from elsewhere," and that *sounds right* next to the verbs we already chose (alchemical on one side, custodial on the other).

That's the whole issue. One pair of words. *(Illustrative only, to show the shape — not a recommendation: something like "forged vs fetched," or a forge-vs-harbor framing. The next session mints for real.)*

## Prompt to paste when you're fresh

> **groom ₣BH** — read the paddock first; it holds every decision from the last session.
>
> Two goals this session, in order:
>
> **1. Mint the made/fetched side-labels.** This is the last open *conversational* vocabulary item. We need a pair of words: one for the side Recipe Bottle *forged itself* (alchemical register — `conjure`/`ordain`), one for the side it *captured from outside* and safekeeps (custodial register — `bank`/`vault`/`garner`/`harbor`/`sheathe`). "Made/fetched" are placeholders. The pair must harmonize with that register split and parse cleanly against the existing nouns (Ark/Lode, Hallmark/Touchmark). Settle it conversationally before anything else — the spec pace needs it.
>
> **2. Then slate the paces.** The model is fully settled; this is decomposition work, not design. Likely sequence: `RBSL` spec first (consumes the side-labels from goal 1), then per-kind capture pipelines, `expel`, the election rework (yoke moves out of Depot `d`), and per-kind test fixtures — the *Test coverage gate* section defines done. Assign each deferred pace-time decision (GAR layout, host-tier regime, GAR provenance attachment, the Lode code-prefix) to the specific pace that owns it. As you cut paces, start bleeding mechanism detail out of the paddock down into dockets — it's at the edge of "read every time."

That's everything teed up. Enjoy the break — sleep is the one context refresh I don't get, and it does more than any of this. Well done today.
